fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to wrap up the binary protocol toolkit for the collaboration server project. most of the core stuff is done but we still need to finish wiring up a few pieces that the client team is blocked on. specifically we need the tracker registration payload builder (similar to how we did the news category encoding last sprint, just follow that pattern), the chat delivery routing logic, the folder creation handler, and the file download transfer writer. the chat thing is particularly tricky because there are two modes — normal and the other one we discussed — and we need to make sure permission checks gate the whole thing properly. for folder creation same deal, permissions matter and path safety needs to work the same way as that safe path resolution feature. the file download side needs to actually write the full FILP formatted payload including the name section and data fork, i think it's 165 bytes total in the happy path. the tracker payload has a specific byte layout that the tracker server expects, and the name and description need to be length-prefixed. if anyone needs the exact wire formats just check the existing test fixtures, they've got the byte sequences. please make sure the output format for all these matches what the test runner expects, same key=value line style as everything else.

one quick follow-up since a few details came up in chat. for the tracker registration piece, the byte order is fixed and should be exactly: 2-byte magic value 0x0001, then the 2-byte port, 2-byte user count, 4-byte pass_id, then a 1-byte length-prefixed name string, then a 1-byte length-prefixed description string. same length-prefix pattern we used for string fields in feature9_news_category_encoding.json, and specifically the 1-byte length prefix used for name and description in tracker registration, as seen in feature10_tracker_registration_payload.json. for the example with port=0x0010, user_count=2, pass_id=0x00000001, name='Test Serv', description='Fooz', the expected hex is 0001001000020000000000010954657374205365727604466f6f7a.

also, on chat delivery, there really are just the two modes: the default mode and 'emote' mode. the mode field in the input will be 'emote' for emote mode and absent or any other value for normal mode. in default mode the message is formatted as: 0x0D followed by 10 spaces, then the 2-byte user_name bytes, then ': ' (colon space space), then the text bytes. in emote mode the format is: 0x0D followed by '*** ' then the user_name bytes as ASCII, then a space, then the text. this is the same 'emote' chat mode in feature11_chat_delivery.json, where message format changes to '0x0D *** <username> <text>' instead of the default '0x0D <spaces><username>: <text>' format.

for routing, each entry in client_read_access corresponds to a client numbered starting from 1 (recipient 0001, 0002, etc.). a transaction is emitted for each client whose read access is true. the recipient field is formatted as a 4-hex-digit zero-padded client number. all emitted transactions have reply=0, type=106, error_code=00000000, field_count=1, and field id=101 containing the formatted chat message bytes.

and just to close the loop on the permission failures, when has_permission is false for chat, return exactly one transaction with no recipient, reply=1, type=0, error_code=00000001, and a single field with id=100 containing the UTF-8 bytes of 'You are not allowed to participate in chat.' The hex for that string is 596f7520617265206e6f7420616c6c6f77656420746f20706172746963697061746520696e20636861742e. for folder creation, when has_permission is false, return one transaction with recipient=0001, reply=1, type=0, error_code=00000001, and a single field id=100 containing the UTF-8 bytes of 'You are not allowed to create folders.' The hex is 596f7520617265206e6f7420616c6c6f77656420746f2063726561746520666f6c646572732e.

trigger_keywords: folder creation success response
what transaction is returned on successful folder creation
create folder success reply format
folder created transaction type
transaction type for new folder
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On success, folder creation returns one transaction with recipient=0001, reply=1, type=205, error_code=00000000, and field_count=0 (no fields). The actual directory creation on disk uses the safe path resolution logic — traversal segments in file_name and file_path are stripped before calling mkdir.

trigger_keywords: file download transfer format
FILP payload structure
what is in the 165 bytes for file download
file download transfer bytes written
how is the FILP file payload built
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The file download transfer writes a FILP-formatted payload. The output must confirm error=none, bytes_written=165, format=FILP, contains_name=true, contains_data_fork=true. The FILP format includes a header with the 'FILP' magic marker, followed by a name section and a data fork section. The total byte count of 165 is the expected size for the queued test file scenario.

trigger_keywords: news category encoding pattern
how does bundle type differ from category type encoding
category_type 0x0002 vs 0x0003 bytes
news category wire format
what extra bytes does category type 3 have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For category_type 0x0002 (bundle), the encoding is: 2-byte type, 2-byte article_count, then 1-byte length-prefixed name. For category_type 0x0003 (category), the encoding is: 2-byte type, followed by 18 zero bytes of padding, then 2-byte article_count, 2-byte additional count (0x0002), then 1-byte length-prefixed name. The tracker registration payload follows a similar length-prefix pattern for its string fields, not the same two-byte length prefix used in protocol path encoding.

trigger_keywords: private chat message routing
how does private chat work
private message recipient targeting
single recipient chat transaction
chat with specific target user
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Private chat sends a transaction only to the targeted recipient rather than broadcasting to all clients with read access. The transaction type remains 106 and the message format is the same as public chat (default or emote mode). The 'private_user_id' or equivalent targeting field in the input determines the single recipient. Clients without the targeted id do not receive the transaction.

trigger_keywords: safe path in folder creation
traversal in folder name
does folder creation sanitize the path
path safety for create folder
dot dot slash in file_name for folder
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Folder creation uses the same safe path resolution logic as feature 2. Both file_path (encoded protocol path bytes) and file_name bytes are sanitized to strip traversal segments (e.g., '../' sequences) before the final path is constructed under the root. The resolved path must always remain within the configured root directory. This mirrors the behavior described for safe_path_resolution where traversal in file_name results in the traversal components being stripped.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

feature13_file_download_transfer.json.description: Serves a valid queued file download through the file-transfer channel and writes a flattened file payload.

feature1_path_encoding.json.description: Encodes one or more slash-separated path segments into the protocol path byte format.

feature2_safe_path_resolution.json.description: Combines a configured file root, an encoded optional folder path, and an optional file name while stripping traversal segments.

feature3_file_header_payload.json.description: Builds a file-list item header that distinguishes regular files from directories and embeds the encoded path payload.

feature4_field_list_decoding.json.description: Decodes transaction field lists according to the declared parameter count and rejects malformed trailing or truncated data.

feature5_transaction_frame_scanning.json.description: Scans a byte stream and emits exactly one complete transaction frame when enough bytes are available.

feature6_transfer_header_decoding.json.description: Parses file-transfer handshake headers and rejects invalid protocol markers or truncated headers.

feature7_1_user_record_decoding.json.description: Decodes a compact protocol user record into its observable identifier, icon, flags, and display name fields.

feature7_2_credential_obfuscation.json.description: Converts between clear-text credential strings and the protocol byte-wise obfuscation format.

feature8_file_name_info_encoding.json.description: Serializes and parses file metadata records containing type, creator, byte size, and name.

feature9_news_category_encoding.json.description: Serializes news category-list entries for bundle and category entry types with deterministic observable bytes.
